informal: Prove that every open set in $\mathbb{R}$ is the union of an at most countable collection of disjoint segments.
lean:
theorem Rudin_exercise_2_29_corrected
    (U : Set ℝ) (hU : IsOpen U) (hConn : IsConnected U) :
    ∃ (f : ℕ → Set ℝ),
      (∀ n, ∃ a b : EReal, f n = {x : ℝ | a < (x : EReal) ∧ (x : EReal) < b}) ∧
      (∀ n, f n ⊆ U) ∧
      (∀ n m, n ≠ m → f n ∩ f m = ∅) ∧
      U = ⋃ n, f n
reasoning: The Lean statement adds the hypothesis `IsConnected U`, which is not present in the informal statement. The informal claim is about *every* open set in ℝ, while the Lean version restricts attention to connected open sets — which are themselves just single open intervals, making the conclusion almost trivial. This is a strict special case of the informal claim, so the Lean theorem is logically weaker. (The ℕ-indexing with allowed empty repeats is a fine way to encode "at most countable", but the spurious connectedness hypothesis is the decisive issue.)
